evidence: All Java class names use PascalCase, all method names use camelCase — standard Java naming conventions followed without drift across all packages.
Package organisation is coherent: api/, config/, query/, utils/, indexer/blacklab/, tagging/, viz/ — each has a single clear responsibility.
ExplorationHandlers and SketchHandlers are symmetric: package-private, constructor-injected, methods named handle*() — consistent handler convention.

Result DTOs split across two locations: ExplorationResult, DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate, ComparisonResult, AdjectiveProfile, Edge are inner public static classes of SemanticFieldExplorer (query package), while WordSketchResult and ConcordanceResult live in a separate QueryResults class (also query package). An acknowledged TODO comment says 'These should be extracted to a dedicated model/ package in a future refactor (see issue 28bc8918 / a430f585)' — recognised outlier.

JSON response fields in the HTTP API mix camelCase (logDice, matchStart, matchEnd, collocateLemma) with snake_case (seed_collocates, seed_collocates_count, relation_type, avg_logdice, shared_by_count, discovered_nouns_count) — two naming conventions coexist in the same response objects.

POST endpoints (radial, bcql) are registered with server.createContext() directly while all GET endpoints use the registerGetHandler() helper — justified by method semantics, not a real convention break.
RelationType enum lives in config/ package; PosGroup enum lives in utils/ — minor inconsistency; both are small enums used by multiple layers.
Test classes consistently use @DisplayName and nested structure (BlackLabSnippetParserTest, SemanticFieldExplorerTest) — uniform test convention.

strengths: Java naming conventions (PascalCase/camelCase) are applied uniformly across all ~20 production source files with zero drift.
Handler class structure is symmetric and consistent: both ExplorationHandlers and SketchHandlers follow identical patterns for construction, method naming, and HTTP utility usage.
Package decomposition is clean and navigable — a new developer can orient quickly.
convention_outlier.issue_character: The main convention outlier is a structural one: result DTOs are hosted as inner classes inside SemanticFieldExplorer rather than in the shared QueryResults class, creating an inconsistent pattern for result type placement that the codebase itself acknowledges as debt; a secondary issue is the HTTP JSON response field naming mixing camelCase and snake_case.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: The Java code-level conventions are very consistent, earning a high partial score. The score is pulled down by two real inconsistencies: the result DTO placement outlier (inner classes in SemanticFieldExplorer vs top-level in QueryResults) and the mixed camelCase/snake_case JSON response keys, which affect the external API contract. These are not trivial style issues — the DTO split creates import coupling and the JSON inconsistency is observable by all API consumers. This places the codebase above 'significant drift' (50) but well below 'fully consistent' (90).

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer hosts result DTOs as inner classes while QueryResults holds sibling result types separately
evidence: ExplorationResult, DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate, ComparisonResult, AdjectiveProfile, Edge, ExploreOptions are public static inner classes of SemanticFieldExplorer
WordSketchResult and ConcordanceResult are top-level nested types in QueryResults.java
ExplorationHandlers imports six inner types explicitly: 'import pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.query.SemanticFieldExplorer.ComparisonResult;' etc — API layer is coupled to SEF internals
Acknowledged TODO comment in SemanticFieldExplorer: 'These should be extracted to a dedicated model/ package in a future refactor (see issue 28bc8918 / a430f585)'
issues.suggestion: Move ExplorationResult and sibling DTOs to QueryResults or a new query/model/ package; update imports in ExplorationHandlers. This resolves the acknowledged TODO and normalises result type placement.

issues.summary: HTTP JSON response objects mix camelCase and snake_case field names in the same response body
evidence: ExplorationHandlers: 'collocMap.put("logDice", ...)' alongside 'response.put("seed_collocates_count", ...)' and 'response.put("relation_type", ...)' — camelCase and snake_case coexist in the same response
SketchHandlers BCQL response: 'resultMap.put("matchStart", ...)' and 'resultMap.put("collocateLemma", ...)' alongside 'response.put("hits", ...)' — inconsistent within the same method
Explore response has: logDice (camelCase), avg_logdice (snake_case), shared_by_count (snake_case) — three naming styles in one response
issues.suggestion: Standardise all JSON response keys to snake_case (the majority convention in the codebase); rename logDice → log_dice, matchStart → match_start, collocateLemma → collocate_lemma consistently across all handlers.

root_causes: SemanticFieldExplorer was built as a self-contained class with its result types embedded, while the simpler QueryResults existed earlier — the two patterns were never unified.
JSON key naming was applied ad-hoc per response, inheriting camelCase from Java field names in some places and switching to snake_case in others.
possible_false_positives: POST-vs-GET handler registration difference (server.createContext vs registerGetHandler) — this is justified by HTTP semantics, not a convention outlier
